title: Apple Launches Core AI for Apple-Silicon Optimized On-Device Generative AI
gist: At WWDC 26 Apple announced Core AI, the successor to Core ML, letting developers run LLMs and generative models (3B to 70B params) entirely on-device across iPhone, iPad, Mac, and Vision Pro with zero server dependency, zero per-token cloud cost — but only on Apple Silicon.

insights.claim: Apple's new Core AI framework, successor to Core ML, provides a unified architecture to deploy models from compact 3B-parameter vision models to large-scale LLMs including up to 70B-parameter reasoning models entirely on-device across iPhone, iPad, Mac, and Vision Pro.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: On-device 70B reasoning models reframe the edge-vs-cloud inference balance; a structural challenge to per-token cloud-inference revenue models.
insights.quote: including reasoning models with up to 70B-parameter reasoning models, across the iPhone, iPad, Mac, and Apple Vision Pro
insights.locator: Apple says the new Core AI framework provides a unified architecture

insights.claim: Core AI runs only on Apple Silicon and delivers user-data privacy, zero server dependencies, and zero per-token cloud costs, positioning Apple's hardware as the moat for 'custom intelligence' applications.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: Tying generative AI to Apple Silicon strengthens hardware lock-in and undercuts cloud-API economics — bullish for Apple's silicon differentiation, a headwind for token-billed providers.
insights.quote: Core AI, which can only run on Apple Silicon, ensures user data privacy, zero server dependencies, and zero per-token cloud costs.
insights.locator: Core AI is the technology underpinning Apple Intelligence

insights.claim: Core AI exposes unified hardware access across CPU, GPU, and Neural Engine under one API, a memory-safe Swift API with zero-copy data paths, and ahead-of-time (AOT) compilation that shifts work off-device for near-instant load times, with PyTorch models convertible via Core AI PyTorch.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: PyTorch conversion path lowers porting friction, easing developer migration of existing models onto Apple's on-device runtime.
insights.locator: Key Core AI capabilities include unified hardware access

insights.claim: Apple now offers three distinct on-device ML approaches — Core ML for classic non-neural ML (decision trees, tabular), Core AI for neural networks and transformers, and MLX Swift for custom model weights (potentially lower performance) — segmenting its AI runtime stack by workload.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: A three-tier runtime strategy clarifies Apple's developer positioning and signals long-term commitment to owning the on-device inference stack.
insights.locator: Apple is providing support for three distinct approaches

insights.claim: Community feedback notes Core AI's long-term value will depend on the future growth of the official Core AI and community ecosystem, an explicit caveat on adoption risk.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: Ecosystem-dependency caveat tempers the on-device hype; adoption is not guaranteed and worth tracking before assuming dominance.
insights.quote: on the the future growth of the official Core AI/community
